section_label: Regulatory & Oversight Bodies, International Relations & War

variables.description: Whether the constitution provides for an Ombudsman (an independent official who investigates complaints against the government)
Whether the constitution contains provisions for a central bank
Policy goals of the central bank as specified in the constitution (asked only if BANK=1)
variables.coding_scheme: 1=Price stability alone; 2=Price stability along with compatible objectives (e.g. banking system stability); 3=Price stability along with potentially conflicting objectives (e.g. full employment); 90=Left to non-constitutional law; 96=Other; 97=Unable to Determine; 98=Not Specified; 99=Not Applicable

variables.description: Whether the constitution mentions a special regulatory body or institution to oversee the media market
Whether the constitution contains provisions for a counter-corruption commission

Whether the constitution includes provisions for the meritocratic recruitment of civil servants (e.g. exams or credential requirements). Includes anti-discrimination or equal opportunity for public employment.
Whether the constitution contains provisions for a human rights commission

Whether the constitution contains provisions for any additional central independent regulatory agencies (beyond counter-corruption commission, human rights commission, central bank, or central election commission)
What additional regulatory institutions are mentioned in the constitution besides those already coded (asked only if EXINST=1)
Whether the constitution contains provisions concerning the relationship between the constitution and international law (any mention of treaties, custom, international obligations, norms suffices)
variables.notes: Borderline; relevant to rule of law when international law is incorporated or given superior status

variables.description: The legal status of treaties vis-à-vis ordinary domestic legislation
variables.coding_scheme: 1=Treaties are superior to ordinary law; 2=Treaties are inferior to ordinary law; 3=Equal status (more recent document is superior); 90=Left to non-constitutional law; 96=Other; 97=Unable to Determine; 98=Not Specified; 99=Not Applicable

variables.description: Who has the power to approve treaties (asked only if TREAT=1). Multi-response.
variables.coding_scheme: Sub-variables (each 0=not selected, 1=selected, 99=not applicable): TREATAP_1=Head of State; TREATAP_2=Head of Government; TREATAP_3=Government/Cabinet; TREATAP_4=First/only Chamber; TREATAP_5=Second Chamber; TREATAP_6=Both Chambers required; TREATAP_90=Left to non-constitutional law; TREATAP_96=Other; TREATAP_97=Unable to Determine; TREATAP_98=Not Specified

variables.description: Whether treaties are reviewable for their constitutionality
Who has the power to declare war. Multi-response.
variables.coding_scheme: Sub-variables (each 0=not selected, 1=selected, 99=not applicable): WAR_1=Head of State; WAR_2=Head of Government; WAR_3=Government/Cabinet; WAR_4=First/only Chamber of Legislature; WAR_5=Second Chamber; WAR_6=Either Chamber; WAR_7=Both Chambers acting jointly; WAR_8=National security council; WAR_90=Left to non-constitutional law; WAR_96=Other; WAR_97=Unable to Determine; WAR_98=Not Specified

variables.description: Who has the power to approve declarations of war
variables.coding_scheme: 1=Head of State; 2=Head of Government; 3=Government/Cabinet; 4=First/only Chamber of Legislature; 5=Second Chamber; 6=Both Chambers required; 7=National security council; 8=No Approval Needed; 90=Left to non-constitutional law; 96=Other; 97=Unable to Determine; 98=Not Specified
